title: Balancing Extensions in Posets of Large Width
speaker: Maxwell Aires
abstract:
A linear extension of P is a linear ordering compatible with the poset relations. Let p(x less than y) be the probability that x
 precedes y in a uniformly random linear extension, and let δ(x,y)=min(p(x less than y),p(y less than x)) and δ(P) be the maximum value of δ(x,y)
 over all x,y in P. The following two conjectures about δ(P)
 are both well-known:
(The "1/3-2/3 Conjecture") δ(P) greater than or equal to 13
 whenever P is not a chain.(The "Kahn-Saks Conjecture") δ(P)→12 as w(P)→∞ (where w(P) is the maximum size of an antichain in P).
While still far from either of these, we prove a number of conditions for δ(P)→12 and δ(P) greater than or equal to 1e−o(1), using a mix of geometric and probabilistic techniques. Joint with Jeff Kahn.

proof_stages.label: Linear-extension probabilities and sorting motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines posets, linear extensions, the number e(P), comparison probabilities, pairwise balancing constants delta(x,y) and delta(P), and motivates them through sorting under partial information.

proof_stages.label: Classical balancing conjectures and rank tools
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the 1/3--2/3 conjecture, recalls the Kahn--Saks 3/11 theorem and the later Felsner--Trotter improvement, and explains the expected rank h(x) of a random linear extension together with the log-concavity idea behind close-rank balancing.

proof_stages.label: Large-width Kahn--Saks conjecture and earlier partial results
proof_stages.summary: After defining width and height, the talk motivates the conjecture that delta(P) tends to 1/2 as width tends to infinity and reviews previous results requiring many minimal elements.

proof_stages.label: New sufficient conditions for near-half and 1/e balance
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated: linear width, logarithmically many minimal elements, or large minimum expected rank imply delta(P)->1/2, while width omega(sqrt n), sublinear height, or superlinear log e(P) imply at least 1/e-o(1) balance, with local antichain and k-point variants.

proof_stages.label: Incomparability, variance, and window parameters
proof_stages.summary: The proof framework introduces pi(x), sigma(x), and window(x), records their implication hierarchy, and proves an antichain window-sum estimate that feeds the width and superlinear-window criteria.

proof_stages.label: Strong Kahn--Saks reduction through variance
proof_stages.summary: A stronger conjecture that large maximum incomparability pi(P) forces delta(P)->1/2 is formulated, and the speaker explains that the original width conjecture would imply it via a bounded-width large-variance balancing theorem and the nontrivial implication pi(P)->infinity implies sigma(P)->infinity.

proof_stages.label: Order-polytope geometry for the 1/e bounds
proof_stages.summary: The proof sketch identifies comparison probabilities with volumes in the order polytope, uses a centroid halfspace theorem to obtain the 1/e bound when expected ranks coincide, and then relaxes equality using the window parameter.

examples.note: A small poset with relations such as w<x, y<x, and y<z is used to list linear extensions, count e(P)=5, and compute a comparison probability of 3/5.

examples.name: Total chain as degenerate balancing case
examples.note: A chain illustrates that when all comparisons are already forced, every pair has balancing constant zero.

examples.name: 2+1 poset and stacked fixed-width obstructions
examples.note: The poset consisting of a two-element chain together with an incomparable element gives balance exactly 1/3, and stacked copies show why fixed-width families cannot force balance tending to 1/2.
examples.engagement_reason: he counts the three extensions of the 2+1 obstruction, derives delta = 1/3, and discusses stacking copies with the same obstruction

passes.reason: the 2+1 poset is the minimal non-chain tight example at δ=1/3, and its stacked width-two versions obstruct convergence to 1/2 without growing width
The 2+1 poset and its fixed-width stacks attain the one-third barrier and obstruct any unqualified asymptotic one-half balancing statement.

examples.name: Logarithmically many minimal elements along a chain
examples.note: A chain x_1<...<x_n with minimal elements y_i placed below x_{2^i} shows that even many minimal elements need not contain a well-balanced pair among themselves, motivating the local antichain caveat and the square-root barrier.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the chain with logarithmically many added minimal elements and reasons through why those minimal elements are poorly balanced while balance is found with the chain

passes.reason: the logarithmically spaced minimal elements along a chain form a tight threshold construction defeating a local minimal-antichain balancing strategy

examples.name: Simple separation posets for window, variance, and incomparability
examples.note: A long chain with one extra element, two equal chains, and a bottom element with many incomparables separate the auxiliary parameters and motivate the nontrivial global implication from large pi(P) to large sigma(P).
examples.engagement_reason: he uses concrete separation posets, such as a long chain plus one element and two equal chains, to compute or estimate window, variance, width, and incomparability behavior
